# Limitations

> The limitations section is where the analyst earns their salary. Read this before the findings.

## Limitations of the data

**1. The cohort is synthetic.** Every child, every session, every environmental record in this repository was generated by `data/data_generation.py`. The synthesis is calibrated to published distributions, but it cannot capture the full structure of real clinical data — particularly autocorrelation across sessions, irregular missingness patterns, and the way real human behavior surprises models. Effects observed here must be replicated on real data before being acted upon.

**2. The synthesis bakes in the hypothesis.** This is the most important limitation. The data-generation script *itself* introduces correlations between environmental covariates and session outcomes. When the analysis "discovers" those correlations, it is partly recovering its own assumptions. This is a useful tool for testing the analytic pipeline (does it find a known signal? what's the smallest signal it can detect?) — but it cannot prove that environmental covariates have these effects in real clinical data. To do so, replicate on real data with no contact between the data generator and the analyst.

**3. Synthetic environmental data is over-clean.** Real weather APIs have outages, real school calendars have last-minute changes, real caregiver check-ins are missing 30% of the time. The synthetic environmental data has minor missingness deliberately introduced (~12% MCAR), but it does not capture the structured missingness that real-world data exhibits.

**4. The cohort is geographically homogeneous by construction.** Synthetic children are assigned to one of five US metro zip codes for weather attribution. Real-world cohorts are far more dispersed. Any geographic clustering effects are not estimable from this data.

**5. No reproduction of real clinical heterogeneity in interventions.** The synthetic data uses six broad intervention types (DTT, NET, group, parent training, sensory, music). Real ABA programs are far more heterogeneous, with hundreds of named protocols. A real-data version of this analysis would need a more granular intervention taxonomy.

## Limitations of the methods

**6. Logistic regression is a strong default but a weak final answer.** The headline models in this analysis are regularized linear models. They are interpretable, fast, and well-calibrated, but they cannot capture interactions or threshold effects without explicit specification. The LightGBM secondary analysis surfaces some non-linearities, but a deeper non-linear analysis (deep learning on the full session stream, recurrent or transformer architectures) is out of scope here and would be the natural next phase.

**7. The hierarchical model assumes exchangeable children.** The Bayesian per-child model treats children as draws from a population distribution with shared hyperparameters. Real children cluster by phenotype, age band, and intervention history — meaning the exchangeability assumption is violated. A more sophisticated model would use a finite mixture or a tree-structured prior, neither of which is implemented here.

**8. We do not adjust for multiple comparisons across the full study.** FDR correction is applied within the environmental predictor block (the pre-registered family of hypotheses). It is not applied across all reported metrics in the paper. Reviewers should treat any non-pre-registered finding with appropriate skepticism.

**9. Treatment integrity is self-reported in the synthesis.** In real ABA work, treatment integrity is measured by external observation; here it is therapist self-report. Real-data integrity scores have lower variance and higher mean than the synthetic ones — meaning a real analysis would have *less* room to attribute outcome variance to TI failures.

**10. Inter-observer agreement is assumed at 85% globally.** In real clinics, IOA varies dramatically by therapist, by skill area, and by phase of training. Sessions coded by lower-IOA therapists carry more measurement error and should be down-weighted, which this analysis does not do.

## Limitations of the framing

**11. The "butterfly effect" framing is rhetorical, not literal.** The analysis does not invoke chaos theory. Effects of environmental covariates are estimated as conditional means under a generalized linear model. The framing emphasizes that small inputs from outside the clinical envelope can have measurable effects on behavior — not that behavioral systems are mathematically chaotic in the Lyapunov-exponent sense.

**12. Causal claims are limited.** This is observational data. Even if real, the associations between environmental covariates and session outcomes do not establish causation. A child whose sessions are scheduled during high-pollen weeks may also have other unobserved differences. Truly causal estimates would require either an instrumental variable design or a randomized re-scheduling experiment, neither of which is feasible here.

**13. Single-clinic generalization is unestablished.** Even if a real-data version of this analysis succeeded at one clinic, the environmental covariate effects might not generalize — different geographies, different climates, different populations all change the relevant variables. The model would need to be re-fit at each deployment site.

**14. The digital twin framing oversells continuous behavioral models.** The prototypes show real-time HRV and cortisol predictions during therapy sessions. In real life, such measurements require wearables that many children won't tolerate. Cortisol in particular cannot be measured continuously; the smoothed trajectory shown in the UI is a model-based smoothing, not a sensor reading. The product copy is careful about this; the analysis text should be too.

## Limitations of the prototypes

**15. The Aura intake flow assumes upload-friendly formats.** Real ABA reports come in dozens of formats — proprietary clinic software exports, scanned faxes, handwritten progress notes. The "drop a PDF and we read it" demo is partially aspirational. Real document parsing would require custom OCR plus per-vendor adapters for the major ABA software platforms (CentralReach, Catalyst, Rethink, etc.).

**16. The "either-way safety net" is a design pattern, not a deployed system.** The Plan B de-escalation kit shown in the prototype is a UI mockup. Building this for real requires therapist training, family co-design, and integration with existing crisis protocols at each clinic. The pattern is sound; the engineering is non-trivial.

**17. The Heptagon visualization is conceptually accurate but operationally simplified.** Real autism data is scattered across far more than seven sources for many children — speech-language sometimes splits into school-based and clinic-based therapists who don't communicate, OT sometimes splits across sensory and motor-planning specialists, behavioral health may include both ABA and non-ABA modalities. The seven-source framing is the modal case, not the universal one.

## What would change my mind

I would update my belief in the headline finding (environmental covariates explain ~16 pts of additional variance) if any of the following occurred:

- A real-data replication at any single clinic showed an effect of <5 percentage points.
- Pre-registered out-of-sample test on a different geography showed coefficients with reversed signs.
- A double-blind analyst replication produced different signs on the two largest effects (substitute therapist, barometric pressure).

I would update my belief in the digital twin framing if:

- Real-time wearable telemetry, in a real clinic for ≥3 months, failed to predict session outcomes better than the static feature model.
- Therapists in a usability study found the forecast irrelevant or overrode it more than 60% of the time.
- Families in a co-design study reported the system felt surveillance-like rather than supportive.

These are the falsification conditions. They are listed here so they constrain me later.

---

*The interesting question is not whether this analysis is right. The interesting question is what it would cost to find out.*
